source_metadata.title_raw: Ty N. Mizote - A-18-137 - July 31, 2018 - Hanford

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 31, 2018


Ty N. Mizote
Griswold, La Salle, Cobb, Dowd & Gin, LLP
111 E. Seventh Street
Hanford, CA 93230

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-18-137

Dear Mr. Mizote:

        This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Hanford City Vice Mayor Sue
Sorensen regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and
not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or
Section 1090.

        Additionally, please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and
accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should
contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

        May the Vice Mayor take part in City Council decisions to place a City-owned building on
the City's surplus property list, including the property's subsequent sale, given that the Vice Mayor
owns a building four blocks more than 1,500 feet from the City-owned building?

CONCLUSION

        Yes. The Vice Mayor may take part in decisions related to placing the City-owned building
on the surplus property list, including the decision to accept offers to purchase the building, because
the sale would not result in a reasonably foreseeable material effect on the Vice Mayor's financial
interests.


_________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-137
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR

Vice Mayor Sue Sorenson was elected to Hanford City Council in November 2016 and took
office in December 2016. (See Sorenson Advice Letter, A-17-076.) The Vice Mayor and her spouse
own a 16-percent interest in "The Laundry Building," a partnership that owns a 17,000 square-foot
professional office building located in historic downtown Hanford. (Ibid.) The downtown area is a
small fraction of non-residential property located in the City. (Ibid.) The Laundry Building is zoned
Downtown Commercial and is leased to commercial tenants: a hair salon, dental lab, dentist, and an
insurance agency. (Ibid.) The property has an approximate value of two-to-three million dollars,
and the partnership has invested a "significant amount" in improving and maintaining the property.
(Ibid.)

The City is considering placing a commercial building on its surplus property list. Both
buildings are located in the downtown Central Parking District. Based upon available maps, the
buildings are separated by four blocks and just over 1,500 feet. The City-owned property is a one
lessee space, currently occupied by Rabobank. Rabobank's lease expired on June 30, 2018.
However, in an e-mail from July 12, 2018, you noted that Rabobank and the City negotiated a five-
year lease extension for the building.

Simon & Hower, Inc. prepared a real estate appraisal, analyzing the impact on Hanford's
downtown commercial real estate market. The appraisal concluded: a sale of the City-owned
building would not affect the value of the Laundry Building in any manner atypical from general
market activity; and a sale of the City-owned building would be based on investment appeal and
have no bearing on surrounding property lease rates. Further, the appraisal noted that "[a]ny lease
negotiations between the City and the and Rabobank will have some type of macro effect on the
downtown Hanford market without specificity to any one property."

The Vice Mayor would like to know whether the Act prohibits her from taking part in
decisions to place the City-owned building on the City's surplus property list, as well as decisions
to accept an offer to purchase the City-owned building. In the event that the City-owned property is
sold, the property will be subject to Rabobank's rights as a tenant.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
(Section 87103.) A conflict of interest may arise only when a reasonably foreseeable financial effect
on a governmental decision on a public official's interest is material. Specific to the facts provided,
Section 87103 identifies the following interests from which a conflict may arise:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)).2

2 "For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the
spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust

File No. A-18-137
Page No. 3

• An interest in real property in which the officer has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000
or more. (Section 87103(b).)

• An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• An interest in your personal finances and those of immediate family members. (See
Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances.³

Additionally, Section 82030(a) defines income to include a pro rata share of any income of
any business in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-
percent interest or greater.

The Vice Mayor has interests in the Laundry Building partnership as a business and a source
of income. In addition, she has an interest in the Laundry Building as real property, in which she
and her husband own a 16-percent interest. Moreover, she has a source of income interest in any
tenant of the Laundry Building from which her pro rata share of income aggregates to $500 or more
in the 12 months prior to a decision.

Foreseeability and Materiality

The standard for foreseeability varies depending on whether an interest is explicitly
involved in the decision.⁴ For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision at
issue, the financial effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can
be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation
18701(b).) The Vice Mayor's interests are not explicitly involved in the decisions at issue.

Business Entity and Source of Income Interests

Regulation 18702.1 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on
an official's interest in a business.⁵ Pertinent to your facts, Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that a
decision's effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably

in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-
percent interest or greater."

³ An effect on an official's interest in his or her personal finances or those of immediate family members is not
considered separately if the decision also affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property. (Regulation
18702.5(c).) Because the decisions at issue also affect the Vice Mayor's business and real property interests, we do not
further analyze her personal financial interests or those of her immediate family.

⁴ An official's interest is explicitly involved in a governmental decision if the interest is a named party in, or
subject of, the decision. An interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest, or if the
decision affects a real property interest described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6). (Regulation 18701(a).)

⁵ Where a source of income is a business entity, Regulation 18702.3(a)(4) refers back to the standard applied in
18702.1 for business entities. Therefore, we do not separately analyze the Vice Mayor's source of income interest
related to the Laundry Building partnership.

File No. A-18-137
Page No. 4

foreseeable that the decision's effect would contribute to a change in the value of the privately-held
business entity.

The Vice Mayor and the City currently utilize both properties as commercial rental
businesses. The decisions at issue would not inhibit the City, the Vice Mayor, or a potential buyer
from continuing to use the City-owned building and the Laundry Building as commercial rental
properties. Additionally, Rabobank and the City have extended the lease of the City-owned
property, and any future sale would be subject to Rabobank's lease. Moreover, it does not appear
that the sale of the City-owned building would have a bearing on surrounding property lease rates,
which include the Laundry Building's leases.6 Therefore, the decision to place the City-owned
building on the surplus property list and, ultimately sell the property, does not appear to contribute
to a change in value of the Vice Mayor's business.

Real Property Interest

For real property not explicitly involved in a decision, Regulation 18702.2(a) establishes
that a foreseeable effect of a governmental decision on an official's financial interest is material if
the decision7:

"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the
market value of the official's property."

Under the facts provided, the City-owned building contains only one tenant and the potential
sale of the building would be subject to the tenants recently executed five-year lease. Moreover, the
City-owned building is four blocks away and more than 1,500 feet from the Laundry Building.
Finally, there is no indication that the sale of the City-owned building will result in an imminent or
significant change in the use of the building, including the appraiser's conclusion that the sale of the
City-owned building would result in "general market activity" effects in the downtown area. Absent
additional facts, there does not appear to be any significant change to the character of the downtown
area that would impact the market value of the Laundry Building. Therefore, the City's decisions do
not appear to have a foreseeable material financial effect on the Vice Mayor's property interest.

Accordingly, the Vice Mayor may take part in decisions to place the City-owned building on
the surplus property list, as well as to accept offers to purchase the City-owned building.

_______________________________

6 The facts provided do not indicate a foreseeable and material effect on the tenants of the Laundry Building
resulting from an effect on their rental space. However, if the Vice Mayor knows or has reason to know that a tenant has
an interest in any other property or business impacted by the decision, the Vice Mayor may be disqualified from the
decision and should seek further advice. (See Regulation 18702.3.)

7 Regulation 18702(b) states: "Notwithstanding Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.5, the financial effect of a
governmental decision is not material if it is nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant."

File No. A-18-137
Page No. 5


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Acting General Counsel

[signature]

By: Sara K. Puricelli
Counsel, Legal Division

SKP:jgl

of a governmental decision on a public official's interest is material. Specific to the facts provided,

percent interest or greater.

QUESTION: May the Vice Mayor take part in City Council decisions to place a City-owned building on 
the City's surplus property list, including the property's subsequent sale, given that the Vice Mayor 
owns a building four blocks more than 1,500 feet from the City-owned building?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The Vice Mayor may take part in decisions related to placing the City-owned building 
on the surplus property list, including the decision to accept offers to purchase the building, because 
the sale would not result in a reasonably foreseeable material effect on the Vice Mayor's financial 
interests., unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR  
Vice Mayor Sue Sorenson was elected to Hanford City Council in November 2016 and took

insurance agency. (Ibid.) The property has an approximate value of two-to-three million dollars, 
and the partnership has invested a "significant amount" in improving and maintaining the property. 
(Ibid.) 
The City is considering placing a commercial building on its surplus property list. Both

year lease extension for the building. 
Simon & Hower, Inc. prepared a real estate appraisal, analyzing the impact on Hanford's
